english: > boundary between the physical matter of the body and the soul Mil.; Was. (272) bem-reg (bem reg) is perh. a mistake in writing, although it also makes sense. — 2. Sch. a pestilential disease; in the Mṅg. bem tol rgyáb-pa (bem tol rgyab pa) seems to denote a surgical operation. — 3. some receptacle, box, bag etc., bém-poí náṅ-nas gser bton (bem po'i nang nas gser bton) she took gold out of the ...?
Cs. calf.
v. be-búm (be bum), Mil.
in Stg. mentioned as a material for clothing; Schr.: 'fine linen', which however is as yet unknown in Tibet.

1. cloak, bér-gyi tú-ba (ber gyi thu ba) tail of the cloak Glr.; ber nág-po (ber nag po) a black cloak Glr. and elsewh.; ǰol-bér ('jol ber) dress with a train Wdk., Pth.; tsem-bér (tsem ber) a cloak patched up of many pieces Pth.; ber-čen (ber chen) gown of a priest, sacerdotal cloak, without sleeves, with gos-čén (gos chen) for a collar; ber-túl (ber thul) fur-cloak. — 2. strength, sharpness, keenness, pungency, of spices, spirits, snuff etc.; ber-càn (ber can) sharp, pungent, piquant; *á-rág-la ber maṅ-po yod* ('a rag la ber mang po yod) the gin is very strong W.; *bér-ra rag, lceí bér-če maṅ-po rag* (ber ra rag, lce'i ber che mang po rag) it bites, burns my tongue; za-bér (za ber) Cs. the burning sensation caused by the stinging of nettles; cf. gár-ba (gar ba).

W., bér-ma (ber ma), ber-lcág (ber lcag) Mil., stick, staff (cf. dbyúg-pa (dbyug pa)); spaí bér-ma (spa'i ber ma) cane, bamboo Mil.; ber-ma lcug gčig (ber ma lcug gcig) a simple staff Mil.; lčags-bér (lcags ber) iron-bar, crow-bar; smyug-bér (smyug ber) cane, walking-stick.
Cs. leather bag.
Ssk., azure stone, lapis lazuli Dzl. wai-dūr-ya dkar-po (bai dU rya dkar po) and sṅon-po (sngon po), v. table of abbreviations.
Ssk., Tib.: rnam-par-snaṅ-mdzad (rnam par snang mdzad), 1. n. of the first Dhyani-Buddha. — 2. a Lotsawa v. Köpp. II., 69.
1. num. figure: 135. — 2. affix, to designate some words as nouns.

wylie: bo tog pa, bo long ba
english: Ts., ankle, ankle-bone.
Cs.: 'n. of a tree, the fruits of which are used as beads for rosaries'.
Ssk., wisdom; also n. of the Indian fig-tree, ficus religiosa, byaṅ-čub-śiṅ (byang chub shing); n. of the white narcissus (Lh.).
prob. pf. of °bó-ba ('bo ba).
W. for bú-mo (bu mo).
ball, for playing Ld.
v. °bógs-pa ('bogs pa).
Sch. roof.

Cs., gain, profit, advantage; bogs °dón-pa (bogs 'don pa) Sch. to yield profit; wherever I met with the word, it was used only in a religious sense: gain for the mind, benefit for the heart, furtherance of devotion of meditation, Mil.

1. also boṅs (bongs), size, dimensions, volume, bulk, boṅ-čé (bong che), -čéṅ (-chen) large, boṅ čé don čuṅ (bong che don chung), large of size, and small of significance are e. g. the lungs (in as far as roasted or boiled they yield little substantial food) Mil.; lus-boṅ-čé (lus bong che), -čuṅ (-chung), briṅ (bring) big, little, middling, as to size of body, S.g.; boṅ-túṅ (bong thung) little in stature; boṅ-tsád (bong tshad), boṅ-tsód (bong tshod) = rdzógs-pa (rdzogs pa) full size, a full-grown body Thgy. — 2. v. boṅ-na (bong na). — 3. also bóṅ-ba (bong ba), Cs.: 'general name for small stones, pebbles etc.'; in medical works ziṅ-gí bóṅ-ba (zing gi bong ba) are mentioned as remedies; in Pth. the word occurs in an enumeration of temporal goods, precluding the above signification. — 4. v. boṅ-bu (bong bu). — 5. provinc. for baṅ (bang) Glr.
Sch. a species of falcon.
v. bóṅ-bu (bong bu).
various species of wolf's bane, aconite, boṅ-dkár (bong dkar), -nág (-nag), -dmár (-dmar), -sér (-ser), used as medicines, or even as poisons.
v. the preceeding and the following article.
Sch. also bóṅ-bo (bong bo), 1. ass, bóṅ-po (bong po) or po-bóṅ (pho bong) he-ass, bóṅ-mo (bong mo) or mo-bóṅ (mo bong) she-ass, boṅ-prúg (bong phrug) colt or foal of an <
